learningGoal: How to recognise common, proper, and abstract nouns (naming words — abstract nouns are things you can't touch, like happiness or fear). You use these every day without even realising!
Why proper nouns need a capital letter — get this right and you'll never lose marks for it

screens.title: What type of word is "dog"?
screens.body:
Look at this sentence:

*"Amira felt happiness when she saw her dog in the park in London."*

Did you know this sentence has **three different types of noun** hiding in it? A **noun** (a naming word) is a word for a person, place, thing, or idea. Can you spot them all?

visualProps.text: Amira felt happiness when she saw her dog in the park in London.
visualProps.label: Three types of noun:

screens.title: Three types of noun
screens.body:
Let's look at each noun in the sentence:

**"Amira felt happiness when she saw her dog in the park in London."**

**dog** — You can see and touch a dog — it's a common noun.
**London** — London is the name of a specific place — it needs a capital letter.
**happiness** — You can't touch happiness — it's a feeling, so it's an abstract noun.

interaction.correctFeedback: Fantastic! Common nouns name everyday things, proper nouns name specific places or people, and abstract nouns name feelings or ideas. You're getting the hang of this!
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly there! Common = everyday things (dog, table). Proper = specific names (London). Abstract = feelings/ideas (happiness, courage). Give it another go!

steps.why: You can see and touch a dog — it's a common noun
London is the name of a specific place — it needs a capital letter
You can't touch happiness — it's a feeling, so it's an abstract noun

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In the sentence:
*"Amira felt happiness when she saw her dog in the park in London."*

What type of noun is **"happiness"**?

interaction.question: What type of noun is "happiness"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! "happiness" is an abstract noun because happiness is a feeling — you can't see it or touch it, so it's abstract. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "happiness" is an abstract noun — happiness is a feeling — you can't see it or touch it, so it's abstract.

visualProps.text: Amira felt happiness when she saw her dog in the park in London.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "happiness"?

screens.title: Three types of noun — you've nailed it!
screens.body: Every noun fits into one of these three groups. Here's your quick guide to tell them apart:

steps.why: dog, chair, school — no capital letter needed

steps.text: Proper noun = the name of a specific person, place, or thing
steps.why: London, Tuesday, Mr Smith — always a capital letter

steps.text: Abstract noun = a feeling or idea
steps.why: love, fear, bravery — you can't touch it ✓

learningGoal: Why proper nouns always need a capital letter — this comes up in the exam all the time!
How to spot when a noun has been wrongly classified

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body:
Someone wrote this sentence:

*"olivia went to bristol on tuesday."*

Something's not right here. Can you spot the mistake? Look carefully!

visualProps.text: olivia went to bristol on tuesday.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: Proper nouns need capital letters!
screens.body:
Olivia, Bristol, and Tuesday are all proper nouns — they name a specific person, city, and day. Proper nouns must start with a capital letter.

The corrected sentence is:

**"Olivia went to Bristol on Tuesday."**

The proper nouns are: **Olivia, Bristol, Tuesday**.

steps.text: WRONG: "olivia went to bristol on tuesday."
steps.why: Missing capital letters on proper nouns

steps.text: RIGHT: "Olivia went to Bristol on Tuesday."
steps.why: Olivia, Bristol, Tuesday are all proper nouns

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which word in this sentence should have a capital letter: "my friend went to cardiff"?

interaction.question: Which word in this sentence should have a capital letter: "my friend went to cardiff"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Cardiff is the name of a specific city, so it's a proper noun and needs a capital letter. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Cardiff" — Cardiff is the name of a specific city, so it's a proper noun and needs a capital letter.

screens.title: The capital letter rule
screens.body: Proper nouns always need a **capital letter**. Here's what counts:

steps.text: Names of people: Amira, Jack, Mrs Patel
steps.why: Specific people = capital letter

steps.text: Names of places: London, Bristol, France
steps.why: Specific places = capital letter
Specific times = capital letter ✓

learningGoal: How to spot action verbs (doing words), being verbs (am, is, are), and helping verbs (words that support the main verb, like have or will) — verbs are the engine of every sentence!
Why every sentence needs at least one verb — without one, it's like a car without wheels

screens.title: How many verbs can you find?
screens.body:
Look at this sentence:

*"Aisha was running because she could hear the bell."*

Did you know every single sentence you've ever read has a **verb** in it? A verb tells you about an action, a state, or what helps the sentence work. This sentence has **three types** hiding in it — can you find them?

visualProps.text: Aisha was running because she could hear the bell.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: Three types of verb
screens.body:
Let's find the verbs in:

**"Aisha was running because she could hear the bell."**

**running** — Running is something you do — it's an action verb.
**was** — 'Was' tells us about a state of being — it's a being verb (is, am, was, are, were).
**could** — 'Could' helps the main verb 'hear' — it's a helping verb (can, could, should, might, will).

interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Action verbs show doing, being verbs show existing (is, was, are), and helping verbs support the main verb.
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Action = doing something (ran, jumped). Being = existing (is, was). Helping = supports another verb (have, will).

steps.why: Running is something you do — it's an action verb
'Was' tells us about a state of being — it's a being verb (is, am, was, are, were)
'Could' helps the main verb 'hear' — it's a helping verb (can, could, should, might, will)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In the sentence:
*"Aisha was running because she could hear the bell."*

What type of verb is **"could"**?

interaction.question: What type of verb is "could"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'could' helps the verb 'hear' — it tells us about ability, so it's a helping verb. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "could" is a helping verb — 'could' helps the verb 'hear' — it tells us about ability, so it's a helping verb.

visualProps.text: Aisha was running because she could hear the bell.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "could"?

screens.title: Three types of verb — brilliant!
screens.body: Every sentence needs a verb — and now you know how to spot all three types:

steps.why: run, write, eat, jump, sing

steps.text: Being verb = am, is, are, was, were
steps.why: They tell us about a state, not an action

steps.text: Helping verb = can, could, should, might, will
steps.why: They help the main verb — never appear alone ✓

learningGoal: How to check a sentence has a verb — the quickest grammar check you'll ever learn!
Why a group of words without a verb is not a sentence

screens.title: Is this a proper sentence?
screens.body:
Tom wrote this:

*"The tall boy with the red backpack."*

It looks like a sentence... but something really important is missing! Can you work out what?

visualProps.text: The tall boy with the red backpack.
visualProps.label: Read carefully:

screens.title: Every sentence needs a verb!
screens.body:
There's no verb — we don't know what the boy is doing or being.

Without a verb, it's just a **fragment** (an incomplete sentence). Watch what happens when we add a verb:

**"The tall boy with the red backpack ran to school."**

Now it's a complete sentence because **"ran"** tells us what happened.

steps.text: Fragment: "The tall boy with the red backpack."
steps.why: No verb = not a sentence
Now we know what happened!

steps.text: Sentence: "The tall boy with the red backpack ran to school."

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Is this a complete sentence?

*"The old castle on the hill."*

interaction.question: Is "The old castle on the hill." a complete sentence?
options: Yes — it has a full stop
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! That's right — this group of words has no verb, so it's not a complete sentence. It needs a doing or being word. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "No — it has no verb" because this group of words has no verb, so it's not a complete sentence. It needs a doing or being word.

visualProps.text: The tall boy with the red backpack.
visualProps.label: Which is correct?

screens.title: The verb rule for sentences
screens.body: A group of words is only a sentence if it has a **verb**. No verb = just a fragment.

steps.why: A doing, being, or helping word

steps.text: No verb = a fragment (not a sentence)
steps.why: 'The tall boy.' — what about him?

steps.text: Add a verb to fix it
steps.why: 'The tall boy ran home.' — now it's complete! ✓

learningGoal: How to tell adjectives (describing words) and adverbs (words that describe how, when, or where something happens) apart — they're easily confused, but you'll learn the trick!
Why adjectives describe nouns (naming words) and adverbs describe verbs (doing words)

screens.title: Adjective or adverb?
screens.body:
Look at this sentence:

*"The tiny kitten purred loudly on the soft blanket."*

**Adjectives** (describing words) describe nouns (things). **Adverbs** describe verbs (actions — how something is done). They both make sentences more interesting — but they do different jobs! Knowing which is which is a real 11+ superpower.

visualProps.text: The tiny kitten purred loudly on the soft blanket.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: Adjectives describe nouns, adverbs describe verbs
screens.body:
In the sentence: **"The tiny kitten purred loudly on the soft blanket."**

**"tiny"** describes the noun **"kitten"** → it's an **adjective**.
**"soft"** describes the noun **"blanket"** → it's an **adjective**.
**"loudly"** describes the verb **"purred"** → it's an **adverb**.

interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Adjectives describe nouns (what kind?) and adverbs describe verbs (how?).
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Adjectives describe nouns (beautiful, enormous). Adverbs describe verbs — many end in -ly (quickly, slowly, carefully).

steps.why: Describes the kitten (a noun)
Describes the blanket (a noun)
Describes how something purred (a verb)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In the sentence:
*"The tiny kitten purred loudly on the soft blanket."*

Is **"loudly"** an adjective or an adverb?

interaction.question: Is "loudly" an adjective or an adverb?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'loudly' describes how the kitten purred — it tells us more about the verb, so it's an adverb. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "loudly" is an adverb — 'loudly' describes how the kitten purred — it tells us more about the verb, so it's an adverb.

visualProps.text: The tiny kitten purred loudly on the soft blanket.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "loudly"?

screens.title: Adjective vs adverb — the easy test
screens.body: Here's a simple trick that works every time:

steps.text: Ask: does it describe a THING?
steps.why: If yes → adjective (the tall tree, a red bus)

steps.text: Ask: does it describe an ACTION?
steps.why: If yes → adverb (ran quickly, sang beautifully)
But not all! 'fast' and 'well' are adverbs too ✓

learningGoal: Why you can't use an adjective (describing word) where an adverb (how/when/where word) belongs
How to fix adjective/adverb mix-ups

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body:
Harry wrote this:

*"Harry ran quick to the shop."*

It sounds almost right... but one sneaky word is wrong. Can you spot it? This mistake catches out grown-ups too!

visualProps.text: Harry ran quick to the shop.
visualProps.label: Read carefully:

screens.title: Adjective used where an adverb belongs!
screens.body:
Harry used 'quick' (an adjective) instead of 'quickly' (an adverb).

'Quick' describes a thing (adjective), but here we need a word to describe how Harry ran (adverb).

The corrected sentence is:
**"Harry ran quickly to the shop."**

steps.why: This is an adjective (describes a thing)
This is an adverb (describes how something is done)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
This sentence has the same type of mistake:

*"She sang beautiful at the concert."*

What should **"beautiful"** be changed to?

interaction.question: What should "beautiful" be changed to?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'beautiful' is an adjective, but we need an adverb to describe how she sang — the correct word is 'beautifully'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct word is "beautifully" — 'beautiful' is an adjective, but we need an adverb to describe how she sang — the correct word is 'beautifully'.

visualProps.text: Harry ran quick to the shop.
visualProps.label: Which is correct?

screens.title: The adjective/adverb swap rule
screens.body: If a word describes **how something is done**, it must be an **adverb** — not an adjective.

steps.why: a quick runner, a neat page
ran quickly, wrote neatly

steps.text: To fix: add -ly to make it an adverb
steps.why: quick → quickly, neat → neatly ✓

learningGoal: How to use pronouns (words like he, she, it, they that replace a noun) to avoid repetition
How to recognise that prepositions (position words like in, on, under, between) show where or when something is

screens.title: Why does this sound odd?
screens.body:
Read this sentence:

*"Poppy put Poppy's bag on the chair. Then Poppy sat beside the chair."*

Yikes — that sounds clunky because the name **Poppy** is repeated too many times! **Pronouns** (words used instead of a name, like 'he', 'she', 'they') fix this. Imagine how annoying conversations would be if we had to say everyone's name every single time!

visualProps.text: Poppy put Poppy's bag on the chair. Then Poppy sat beside the chair.
visualProps.label: This sentence sounds clunky:

screens.title: Pronouns replace nouns, prepositions show position
screens.body:
Look how pronouns make the sentence better:

**"Poppy put her bag on the chair. Then she sat beside it."**

**"her"** replaces **"Poppy's"**
**"she"** replaces **"Poppy"**
**"it"** replaces **"the chair"**

And the **prepositions** (words that show position or time): **"on"** shows where the bag is (position), **"beside"** shows where she sat (position).

interaction.sentence: A pronoun replaces a ____ to avoid repetition
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Pronouns like "he", "she", "it", and "they" replace nouns so we don't keep repeating names. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — a pronoun replaces a NOUN. Instead of saying "Daisy" over and over, we say "she".

steps.why: Pronoun — avoids repeating the name
Pronoun — avoids repeating the name
Preposition — tells us where or when
Preposition — tells us where or when

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In the sentence:
*"Poppy put her bag on the chair. Then she sat beside it."*

What type of word is **"beside"**?

interaction.question: What type of word is "beside"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'beside' shows where Poppy sat in relation to the chair — it tells us position, so it's a preposition. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "beside" is a preposition — 'beside' shows where Poppy sat in relation to the chair — it tells us position, so it's a preposition.

visualProps.text: Is "beside" a pronoun or a preposition?
visualProps.label: Which word class?

screens.title: Pronouns and prepositions — well done!
screens.body: Great work! These two word types do very different jobs:

steps.why: he, she, it, they, we, his, her, them
in, on, at, under, behind, before, after

steps.text: Quick test: can it replace a name?
steps.why: Yes → pronoun. No → probably a preposition ✓

learningGoal: Why using the wrong pronoun (a word like he, she, it, they) causes confusion
How to check a pronoun matches the noun it replaces

screens.title: Can you spot the pronoun mistake?
screens.body:
Someone wrote this:

*"Lucas told Maya that he needed his book back, but she said it was hers. Then he lost them."*

The pronouns don't match properly. Can you spot what's wrong?

visualProps.text: Lucas told Maya that he needed his book back, but she said it was hers. Then he lost them.
visualProps.label: Read carefully:

screens.title: Pronouns must match!
screens.body:
The mistake: Lucas used 'them' at the end, but it should be 'it' because he's talking about one book.

'them' is a plural pronoun (for more than one thing), but there's only one book — so we need 'it'.

Corrected: **"Lucas told Maya that he needed his book back, but she said it was hers. Then he lost it."**

steps.why: Doesn't match the noun it replaces
Now it matches!

steps.text: Pronouns must match in number and gender

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
What's wrong with this sentence?

*"Ella and her friend went to the park. He had a great time."*

What should **"He"** be?

interaction.question: What should "He" be changed to?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! the sentence is about Ella AND her friend (two people), so we need 'they' not 'he'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "They" because the sentence is about Ella AND her friend (two people), so we need 'they' not 'he'.

visualProps.text: Lucas told Maya that he needed his book back, but she said it was hers. Then he lost them.
visualProps.label: Which is correct?

screens.title: Pronoun matching rules
screens.body: A pronoun must match the noun it replaces. Check these three things:

steps.why: One dog = it, three dogs = them
Match the person you're talking about

steps.text: Read it back: does it make sense?
steps.why: If it sounds odd, the pronoun is probably wrong ✓

learningGoal: How to recognise that conjunctions (joining words like and, but, because) join words, phrases, or sentences
How to spot determiners (words like the, a, this, some that sit before a noun)

screens.title: Two small but mighty word types!
screens.body:
Look at this sentence:

*"Phoebe wanted the cake and the biscuit, but she could only pick some of them because her mum said every treat costs money."*

Did you know some of the smallest words in English do the biggest jobs? **Conjunctions** (joining words like 'and', 'but', 'because') connect parts of a sentence. **Determiners** sit before nouns and tell us *which one* or *how many*.

visualProps.text: Phoebe wanted the cake and the biscuit, but she could only pick some of them because her mum said every treat costs money.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: Conjunctions join, determiners specify
screens.body:
In the sentence: **"Phoebe wanted the cake and the biscuit, but she could only pick some of them because her mum said every treat costs money."**

**Conjunctions** (joining words):
"and" — joins two things Phoebe wanted (cake and biscuit)
"but" — introduces a contrast (she wanted both, but could only pick some)
"because" — gives the reason (why she could only pick some)

**Determiners** (words before a noun that tell which one):
"the" before "cake" — tells us which cake — a specific one
"some" before "them" — tells us how many — not all of them
"every" before "treat" — means all of them, each one

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Conjunctions join ideas, determiners sit before nouns, and the type depends on whether the ideas are equal. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by finding joining words (conjunctions), then words before nouns (determiners).

steps.why: joins two things Phoebe wanted (cake and biscuit)
introduces a contrast (she wanted both, but could only pick some)
Sits before "cake" — tells us which cake — a specific one
Sits before "treat" — means all of them, each one

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In the sentence:
*"Phoebe wanted the cake and the biscuit, but she could only pick some of them because her mum said every treat costs money."*

What type of word is **"because"**?

interaction.question: What type of word is "because"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'because' joins two parts of the sentence and gives a reason — it's a conjunction. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "because" is a conjunction — 'because' joins two parts of the sentence and gives a reason — it's a conjunction.

visualProps.text: Phoebe wanted the cake and the biscuit, but she could only pick some of them because her mum said every treat costs money.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "because"?

screens.title: Conjunctions and determiners — sorted!
screens.body: Brilliant work! Here's the simple way to tell them apart:

steps.why: and, but, because, so, or, although

steps.text: Determiner = sits right before a noun
steps.why: a, the, some, many, every, this, those

steps.text: Quick test: does it join two things?
steps.why: Yes → conjunction. Does it come before a noun? → determiner ✓

learningGoal: How to pick the right conjunction (joining word) for the meaning
Why 'and', 'but', and 'because' are not interchangeable

screens.title: Can you spot the wrong conjunction?
screens.body:
Jasmine wrote this:

*"Jasmine was tired and she kept working."*

The sentence makes sense grammatically, but the **conjunction** gives the wrong meaning. Can you spot which one?

visualProps.text: Jasmine was tired and she kept working.
visualProps.label: Read carefully:

screens.title: Different conjunctions, different meanings!
screens.body:
Jasmine used 'and' when 'but' would be better, because the two parts contrast (tired vs kept working).

'And' joins things that go together. 'But' joins things that contrast. Being tired and keeping working are opposites, so 'but' fits.

The corrected sentence:
**"Jasmine was tired but she kept working."**

steps.why: Gives the wrong meaning
Now the meaning is clear

steps.result: Pick the one that matches the meaning!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
This sentence uses the wrong conjunction:

*"The film was boring and I turned it off."*

What should **"and"** be changed to?

interaction.question: What should "and" be changed to?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! the boring film is the reason for turning it off — 'so' shows a result, which fits better than 'and'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The best conjunction here is "so" — the boring film is the reason for turning it off — 'so' shows a result, which fits better than 'and'.

visualProps.text: Jasmine was tired and she kept working.
visualProps.label: Which is correct?

screens.title: Choosing the right conjunction
screens.body: Each conjunction has a specific job. Pick the one that matches the **meaning** you want:

steps.why: I like cake and biscuits
I was tired but I kept going

steps.text: "because" = giving a reason, "so" = showing a result
steps.why: I was tired because I ran. I was tired so I sat down. ✓

subConcepts.name: Words That Change Class in Context

learningGoal: How to understand that the same word can be a different word class (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) in different sentences — this blows people's minds!
How to use the 'function test' — ask what job the word is doing right now

screens.title: One word, three different jobs!
screens.body:
The word **"light"** can be used in completely different ways — how cool is that?

1. *"Please switch on the light."*
2. *"This bag is very light."*
3. *"Can you light the candle?"*

Same word, but the **job it does** changes every time. In the 11+ exam, you need to work out what job the word is doing in THAT sentence.

visualProps.text: Please switch on the light.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "light" here?

screens.title: The Function Test — what job is the word doing?
screens.body:
Let's test the word **"light"** in each sentence:

**"Please switch on the light."** — Here 'light' is a thing you can switch on — it's a noun.
**"This bag is very light."** — Here 'light' describes the bag (not heavy) — it's an adjective.
**"Can you light the candle?"** — Here 'light' is an action (setting fire to something) — it's a verb.

The trick: don't think about what the word *usually* is. Think about what job it's doing **right now**.

steps.why: Here 'light' is a thing you can switch on — it's a noun
Here 'light' describes the bag (not heavy) — it's an adjective
Here 'light' is an action (setting fire to something) — it's a verb

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In this sentence:
*"This bag is very light."*

What word class is **"light"**?

interaction.question: What word class is "light" in this sentence?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'light' describes the bag — it tells us the bag is not heavy, so it's doing the job of an adjective. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Adjective" — 'light' describes the bag — it tells us the bag is not heavy, so it's doing the job of an adjective.

visualProps.text: This bag is very light.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "light" here?

screens.title: The Function Test — your go-to strategy!
screens.body: Many English words can be different word classes — and now you know how to handle them. The exam loves testing this! Here's your strategy:

steps.text: Step 1: Read the whole sentence carefully
steps.why: Context is everything!

steps.text: Step 2: Ask — what job is this word doing?
steps.why: Is it naming, describing, doing, or showing position?

steps.text: Step 3: Match the job to the word class
steps.why: Naming = noun, describing a thing = adjective, doing = verb, describing an action = adverb ✓

learningGoal: How to identify the word class (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) of the SAME word in different sentences
Why context changes everything about a word's class

screens.title: Is "play" always a verb?
screens.body: Someone said 'play' is always a verb. But is that really true?

bodyParts.content: Someone said 'play' is always a verb. Look at these two sentences:

props.text: The children play football every Saturday.
props.label: "play" as a verb:

props.text: We went to see a play at the theatre.
props.label: "play" as a noun:

screens.title: Context changes the word class!
screens.body:
That's wrong! In 'We went to see a play', the word 'play' is a noun — it's a thing you can watch at the theatre.

Here's the proof:

**"The children play football every Saturday."** → **verb**
**"We went to see a play at the theatre."** → **noun**

Always check what job the word is doing in **that particular sentence**.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Find the word, ask what job it's doing, and check the context for clues. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by finding the word, then ask what job it's doing in the sentence.

steps.why: "play" is a verb here

steps.text: "We went to see a play at the theatre."
steps.why: "play" is a noun here

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
What word class is **"play"** in this sentence?

*"Ella scored a brilliant play in the netball match."*

interaction.question: What word class is "play" in this sentence?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'a play' is a thing (a move or action in a game) — you can put 'a' before it, which shows it's a noun. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Noun" — 'a play' is a thing (a move or action in a game) — you can put 'a' before it, which shows it's a noun.

visualProps.text: Ella scored a brilliant play in the netball match.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "play" here?

screens.title: Never assume — always check!
screens.body: The exam often gives you a word and asks what class it is **in that sentence**. Now you know the trick — here's your checklist:

steps.text: Don't assume! Read the sentence first
steps.why: 'play' can be a noun OR a verb

steps.text: Ask: is it naming, doing, or describing?
steps.why: That tells you the word class

steps.text: Check: can you put 'a' or 'the' before it?
steps.why: If yes, it's probably a noun in this sentence ✓

learningGoal: How to recognise five types of pronoun: personal (I, you, he), possessive (my, your, his), relative (who, which, that), demonstrative (this, that, these, those), and interrogative (who?, what?, which?)
How to see that each type does a different job in a sentence

screens.title: Not all pronouns are the same!
screens.body:
Look at this sentence:

*"She told me that the book which was on the shelf was hers."*

Did you know this sentence has **three different types of pronoun** hiding in it? Pronouns all replace or refer to nouns, but each type does it in a different way.

visualProps.text: She told me that the book which was on the shelf was hers.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: Three pronoun types in one sentence
screens.body:
Let's look at each pronoun in:

**"She told me that the book which was on the shelf was hers."**

**She** — 'She' is a personal pronoun — it replaces a person's name (Aisha).
**me** — 'me' is also a personal pronoun — it replaces my name.
**hers** — 'hers' is a possessive pronoun — it shows who owns the book (no apostrophe!).
**which** — 'which' is a relative pronoun — it starts a clause that tells us more about the book.

interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Personal pronouns replace names (I, he, she). Possessive show ownership (his, mine). Relative link clauses (who, which, that).
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Personal = replace names (I, he, she, they). Possessive = show ownership (his, mine, theirs). Relative = link clauses (who, which, that).

steps.why: 'She' is a personal pronoun — it replaces a person's name (Aisha)
'hers' is a possessive pronoun — it shows who owns the book (no apostrophe!)
'which' is a relative pronoun — it starts a clause that tells us more about the book

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In the sentence:
*"She told me that the book which was on the shelf was hers."*

What type of pronoun is **"hers"**?

interaction.question: What type of pronoun is "hers"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'hers' shows ownership — the book belongs to her — and it doesn't need an apostrophe. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "hers" is a possessive pronoun — 'hers' shows ownership — the book belongs to her — and it doesn't need an apostrophe.

visualProps.text: She told me that the book which was on the shelf was hers.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "hers"?

screens.title: Five types of pronoun — you know the key three!
screens.body: The 11+ often asks you to name the **type** of pronoun. Here are the three most important ones to remember:

steps.text: Personal: I, you, he, she, it, we, they, me, him, her, us, them
steps.why: Replace a person or thing's name

steps.text: Possessive: mine, yours, his, hers, its, ours, theirs
steps.why: Show ownership — NEVER use an apostrophe!

steps.text: Relative: who, which, that, whose, where, when
steps.why: Start a clause giving more information about a noun ✓

learningGoal: How to identify demonstrative pronouns (this, that, these, those) and interrogative pronouns (who?, what?, which?)
How to tell pronoun types apart in tricky sentences

screens.title: Pointing and asking — two more pronoun types!
screens.body:
Look at this sentence:

*"Who left these on the table? That is not where they belong!"*

**Demonstrative pronouns** point to specific things: **this, that, these, those**.
**Interrogative pronouns** ask questions: **who, what, which**.

visualProps.text: Who left these on the table? That is not where they belong!
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: The five pronoun types
screens.body:
There are **five types** of pronoun:

**Personal:** I, you, he, she, it, we, they — replace a person or thing.
**Possessive:** mine, yours, his, hers, theirs — show ownership.
**Relative:** who, which, that — add extra information.
**Demonstrative:** this, that, these, those — point to specific things.
**Interrogative:** who, what, which — ask questions.

Now spot them in: **"Who left these on the table? That is not where they belong!"**

steps.why: 'Who' asks a question — it's an interrogative pronoun
'these' points to specific things nearby — it's a demonstrative pronoun
'That' points to a specific thing — it's a demonstrative pronoun
'they' replaces the noun for the things on the table — it's a personal pronoun

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In the sentence:
*"Who left these on the table? That is not where they belong!"*

What type of pronoun is **"these"**?

interaction.question: What type of pronoun is "these"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'these' points to specific things (the ones on the table) — demonstrative pronouns point to particular things: this, that, these, those. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "these" is a demonstrative pronoun — 'these' points to specific things (the ones on the table) — demonstrative pronouns point to particular things: this, that, these, those.

visualProps.text: Who left these on the table? That is not where they belong!
visualProps.label: What type of word is "these"?

screens.title: Quick recap!
screens.body: Five pronoun types — ask yourself what the word is doing:

steps.why: I, you, he, she, it, we, they

steps.text: Possessive pronouns show who owns something
steps.why: mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs
who, which, that — they link parts of a sentence
this, that, these, those
who, what, which — they start a question! ✓

learningGoal: How to tell coordinating conjunctions (joining words for equal parts: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So — FANBOYS) from subordinating conjunctions (joining words that start a dependent part: because, although, when)
Why coordinating conjunctions join equal parts and subordinating conjunctions create dependent clauses (parts that can't stand alone)

screens.title: FANBOYS vs the rest!
screens.body:
Look at this sentence:

*"Aisha likes maths and science, but she finds spelling tricky because she started learning English late."*

Some conjunctions join **equal parts** — these are called **coordinating** conjunctions. Others make one part **depend on** another — these are **subordinating** conjunctions.

Here's a handy trick to remember the coordinating ones: **FANBOYS** (For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So). Once you know FANBOYS, everything else is subordinating!

visualProps.text: Aisha likes maths and science, but she finds spelling tricky because she started learning English late.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: Coordinating joins equals, subordinating creates dependence
screens.body:
In the sentence:

**"Aisha likes maths and science, but she finds spelling tricky because she started learning English late."**

**"and"** — joins two equal things Aisha likes (maths + science) (coordinating — FANBOYS).
**"but"** — joins two equal clauses that contrast (likes maths and science vs finds spelling tricky) (coordinating — FANBOYS).
**"because"** — introduces a reason that depends on the main clause — 'because she started learning English late' can't stand alone (subordinating).

Test: **"because she started learning English late"** cannot be a sentence on its own. It *depends on* **"she finds spelling tricky"**.

interaction.sentence: FANBOYS (For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So) are all ____ conjunctions
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! FANBOYS are all coordinating conjunctions — they join two equal ideas together. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — FANBOYS (For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So) are COORDINATING conjunctions. They join equal ideas.

steps.why: joins two equal things Aisha likes (maths + science)
joins two equal clauses that contrast (likes maths and science vs finds spelling tricky)
introduces a reason that depends on the main clause — 'because she started learning English late' can't stand alone

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
In the sentence:
*"Aisha likes maths and science, but she finds spelling tricky because she started learning English late."*

What type of conjunction is **"because"**?

interaction.question: What type of conjunction is "because"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'because' introduces a dependent clause (one that can't stand alone as a sentence) — it's subordinating. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "because" is a subordinating conjunction — 'because' introduces a dependent clause (one that can't stand alone as a sentence) — it's subordinating.

visualProps.text: Aisha likes maths and science, but she finds spelling tricky because she started learning English late.
visualProps.label: What type of word is "because"?

screens.title: FANBOYS — your secret weapon!
screens.body: The 11+ loves asking about conjunction types, and now you have the ultimate cheat code:

steps.why: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So — that's ALL of them!
because, although, when, while, if, since, unless, until, before, after

steps.text: The dependence test: can the clause stand alone?
steps.why: If not, the conjunction is subordinating ✓

learningGoal: How to identify clause structure with conjunctions (joining words)
Why subordinate clauses (dependent parts that need the main sentence) can't stand alone as sentences

screens.title: Is this a complete sentence?
screens.body:
Grace's teacher marked this as wrong:

*"Because it was raining."*

It has a capital letter and a full stop — so why isn't it a proper sentence?

visualProps.text: Because it was raining.
visualProps.label: Read carefully:

screens.title: Subordinate clauses can't stand alone!
screens.body:
A subordinate clause needs a main clause to be a complete sentence. 'Because it was raining' tells us a reason, but doesn't tell us what happened.

Here's how to fix it — add a main clause:

**"We stayed inside because it was raining."**

Now **"We stayed inside"** is the main clause (it makes sense alone), and **"because it was raining"** is the subordinate clause (it depends on the main clause).

steps.why: This is only a subordinate clause — it can't be a sentence
It makes the clause depend on something else

steps.text: RIGHT: "We stayed inside because it was raining."
steps.result: Now it has a main clause + a subordinate clause

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Is this a complete sentence?

*"Although the sun was shining."*

interaction.question: Is "Although the sun was shining." a complete sentence?
options: Not a sentence — it's only a subordinate clause
Not a sentence — it has no verb
Not a sentence — it has no noun
Yes — it has a full stop
interaction.correctAnswer: Not a sentence — it's only a subordinate clause
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Although the sun was shining' is a subordinate clause — it needs a main clause like 'we took an umbrella' to be complete. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Although the sun was shining' is a subordinate clause — it needs a main clause like 'we took an umbrella' to be complete.

visualProps.text: Because it was raining.
visualProps.label: Which is correct?

screens.title: Main clause + subordinate clause = complete sentence
screens.body: A subordinate clause **depends** on a main clause. It can't be a sentence on its own. Here's how to check:

steps.text: Main clause = makes sense on its own
steps.why: 'We stayed inside.' — yes, that's a complete sentence

steps.text: Subordinate clause = depends on something else
steps.why: 'Because it was raining.' — raining caused what? We need more!

steps.text: Spot the conjunction to find the subordinate clause
steps.why: because, although, when, while, if, unless, until, before, after ✓
